data.title: 4 Upcoming Dividend Increases
data.description:
Discover the power of dividend stocks, your ticket to consistent and growing income. What a wonderful feeling it is to sit back and collect regular paychecks from everyday companies just by holding their shares. Embrace a strategy that helps your returns keep rising and your financial future look brighter. Now is a great time to invest in dividend stocks and reap the rewards they can offer.

Companies that regularly raise their dividends tend to be great long-term investments. Dividend increases suggest financial strength and stability, indicating that a company can generate reliable cash flow. It also requires deliberate capital allocation planning by management to make it a core tenet of the business. Consistent dividend growth typically indicates that a company is generating reliable cash flow, which in turn enables it to offer investors a combination of income and capital appreciation.

From the broader universe of investable stocks, I focus on those with a proven track record of annual dividend increases. I watch dividend increase announcements and combine those with other supporting data points. This information is core to my investment process, and I share it here with the Seeking Alpha community to help you make more informed investment decisions.

This week, I'll spotlight another group of companies and provide more detail on both the selections and the methodology behind them.

A few highlights from this week's list:

  • Average dividend increase: 6.6% (median: 6.7%)

  • Average dividend streak: 18 years (median: 15 years)

  • This week consists of all financial stocks!

My strategy focuses on buying, holding, and adding to positions in companies that raise their dividends annually and have the potential to outperform relevant benchmarks over time.

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here is the result of combining multiple data sources: the "U.S. Dividend Champions" spreadsheet provides the universe of companies I review, and then upcoming dividend announcements. This combination joins data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth and the timeliness of their dividend increases. It's important to note that every company on this list has a minimum of 5 years of dividend growth history.

To be considered for this list, a company must offer higher total annual dividends. Although a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories that I'll use throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years.
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years.
  • Contender: 10-24 years.
  • Challenger: 5+ years.
Category Count
King 0
Champion 1
Contender 2
Challenger 1

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
Capital City Bank Group (CCBG) 12 2.41 8-Sep-25 8.33% Contender
Broadridge Financial Solutions, Inc. (BR) 18 1.57 11-Sep-25 10.80% Contender
Community Financial System, Inc. (CBU) 33 3.17 12-Sep-25 2.17% Champion
Silvercrest Asset Management Group Inc. (SAMG) 8 5.25 12-Sep-25 5.00% Challenger

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date by which you must own the stock to receive the dividend.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
CCBG 0.24 0.26 8.33%
BR 0.88 0.98 10.80%
CBU 0.46 0.47 2.17%
SAMG 0.2 0.21 5.00%

Additional Metrics

Here are additional metrics related to these companies. Some data points include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52 Week Low 52 Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
CCBG 43.22 32.38 44.04 13.05 33% Off Low 2% Off High
BR 249.84 206.95 271.91 36.19 21% Off Low 8% Off High
CBU 59.35 49.44 73.39 21.24 20% Off Low 19% Off High
SAMG 15.99 13.54 19.2 11.47 18% Off Low 17% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the "Chowder Rule," the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
SAMG 5.25 5.3 5.6 5.2 5.3 10.3
CBU 3.17 2.2 2.3 2.3 4.4 5.6
CCBG 2.41 14.6 13.7 11.7 23.9 14.1
BR 1.57 10 11.2 10.3 12.6 11.8

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and grow their dividend payouts over time. For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

SCHD features strong historical performance, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends. With over $69B in assets, it's an incredibly popular dividend-growth ETF. The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four key factors that SCHD tracks in its index.

Based on this analysis (and further due diligence), I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

YCharts was having issues this week, so I went back to my old stock return calculator.

For reference, SCHD returned about 220%, or 12.3% per year. The top dog of the group was Broadridge Financial, with a 480% total return (19.2% CAGR).

CCBG was the only other one that managed to outperform SCHD with a 253% total return (13.4% annualized).

The last two in the group were CBU at 124% and SAMG at 111%. Unfortunately, these last two significantly underperformed SCHD.

Next Steps

I'm selective about the individual holdings in my portfolio. In my individual sleeve portion of my portfolio, I have 33 holdings. So, the bar is pretty high due to diminishing returns of adding an additional holding. I'm looking for companies that offer significant alpha over SCHD and stand out from it. Furthermore, I believe in the importance of consistent, strong earnings and dividend growth over time. These articles provide me (and I hope you) a chance to review what's performed well and to take a closer look at my current holdings.

Last week, I decided to add Unity to my watchlist as I learn more about the company. This week, I want to take another look at Broadridge Financial. I've looked at them before, and I'm intrigued with their historical performance. But I want to make sure forward-looking performance seems strong as well.

I'd love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

data.title: Northwest Natural Has Value, Growth, And Yield
data.description:
Successful investing doesn’t have to be a popularity contest. In fact, some of my best picks are ones off the beaten path and can be found in both bull and bear markets alike. That’s because it’s a market for stocks rather than the stock market, and there is always something on sale due to market irrationality. Sooner or later, the market pendulum will swing back toward quality dividend stocks that are undervalued, and patient investors will reap the reward while getting paid a solid income stream in the meantime.

This brings me to Northwest Natural Holding Company (NWN), which I last covered in June, noting its mix of value and income and strategic expansion. What’s great for value investors is that NWN has been rangebound, and currently trades just 0.44% higher than when I last covered it, offering opportunities to layer into the stock. As shown below, NWN currently trades in the middle of its 52-week range while sporting a 4.7% yield.

In this article, I revisit NWN including recent business fundamentals, and discuss what makes it an appealing value and income stock in this frothy market, so let’s get started!

Why NWN?

Northwest Natural Holding Company is a diversified utility based in Portland Oregon. It has roots dating back to the 1800s and has 69 consecutive years of dividend payments. NWN’s business is broken into Natural Gas Utility, Water, Renewables, and most recently SiEnergy, a high-growth utility in Texas. Notably, NWN’s Texas footprint was expanded further this year with the acquisition of Hughes Gas Resources, which was rebranded as Pines Holdings.

NWN continued to deliver strong results during Q2 2025. This included gas utility margin rising by $17 million due to a new rate implemented in November of last year in Oregon with a 9.5% allowed ROE. Segment net income rose by $4.5 million, partially offset by higher O&M (operating & maintenance) and depreciation. At the same time, NWN’s water utilities added $1.8 million (or $0.04 per share) in net income due to new rates in Arizona, contributions from acquired utilities, and customer growth of 6%.

Renewables continues to provide steady income, as its RNG (renewable natural gas) platform is backed by long-term, fixed-price contracts with investment grade rated counterparties. Importantly, SiEnergy contributed $1 million in net income (or $0.03 per share) during the second quarter. This was driven by both organic growth and the Pines acquisition, which added to the backlog and number of customers. Pines alone added 7,000 connections northeast of Houston and has a robust backlog of 12,000 meters. This contributed to robust 10.6% YoY customer growth for all of NWN’s utilities.

Management is guiding for 5% EPS growth annually at the midpoint of the range over the medium term. NWN is also expected to spend $450 to $500 million in capital expenditures this year on grid modernization, meter replacements, and storage reinforcement. Over the medium term, NWN also benefits from population inflows into Texas, which gives its gas utility there multi-year growth visibility. Plus, NWN’s water utilities offer consolidation potential across the sector as communities increasingly rely on private operators to fund infrastructure upgrades.

NWN carries a strong balance sheet with an A- credit rating from S&P. This is supported by a safe FFO to debt target of 14% and $550 million of liquidity, including $103 million of cash on hand. Importantly for income investors, NWN carries a respectable 4.7% dividend yield that’s well-covered by a 65% payout ratio, leaving plenty of retained capital to fund growth.

Lastly, I continue to see value in NWN at the current price of $41.35 with a forward PE of 14.2, sitting far below its historical PE of 19.8, as shown below.

Sell side analysts who follow the company estimate 5-7% annual EPS growth over the next two years. I believe this is reasonable due to organic customer growth, base rate increases, and territory expansion, including its backlog in Texas. This, combined with the near-5% dividend yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation, could result in market-beating total returns from here.

Risks to NWN include the threat of wildfires due to a leak or rupture in gas pipelines, especially when it comes to forested areas of Oregon and Washington. Cheaper electricity due to low-cost solar could compete with natural gas for home heating. Improved customer energy efficiency, such as heat pumps, could reduce natural gas demand.

Investor Takeaway

Northwest Natural Holding stands out as a compelling value and income play in a volatile market. It has a long dividend growth track record and a well-covered 4.7% yield. This is backed by regulated utility operations and expansion into high-growth areas like Texas through SiEnergy and Pines Holdings. NWN’s recent performance, including rate-driven margin gains and strong customer growth across all segments, supports potential 5-7% EPS growth annually. Lastly, trading at a forward PE well below its historical average, NWN offers patient investors an attractive entry point with potential for market-beating total returns from here.

data.title: What Will Light A Fire Under Small Caps?
data.description:
By Jeff Weniger

The house of pain continues with small caps, at least on a relative basis. Year-to-date, the S&P 600 index has posted a 3.0% gain, so it's not like money is being burned. But still, even the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond index is up 4.9% YTD and the S&P 500 is up 10.9%.1

BofA's fund flow data tells a thousand words.

Figure 1: A Tale of Two Asset Classes

We're at a moment of truth with large versus small, too. When I plot the S&P 100 relative to the big, broad Wilshire 5000 index, it is making its third attempt at resistance levels that date to the 1990s. If recent action holds, this would mark an important breakout for large caps.

Figure 2: A Moment of Truth

Because of small caps' multi-year struggle, the group is trading at a 21.8% discount to large caps on reported earnings (figure 3). It doesn't mean a small-cap resurgence will start tomorrow, but I do find solace in these valuation gaps resembling the situation that manifested in both the mid-1970s and the turn of the century.

Figure 3: Small Caps' Relative Valuations Are Promising

Small caps are cheap, at least relatively, but there is a potential 2026 headwind that we should address.

The S&P 600 index of small caps has an easy hurdle this calendar year, in that the Street is penciling in earnings growth of just 2.7% to the S&P 500's 7.4%, according to Yardeni Research. But for next year, the Street has S&P 600 earnings jumping another 18.8% (the S&P 500 is expected to see earnings growth of 13.9%). Those will probably both be revised down, as usual, but it's still an easier hurdle for large caps than small caps.

As for the Dollar

It's one of the most common questions we get on large versus small. I wish I had some strong view here, but I don't. The problem with the dollar is you must get two things correct to make money:

  • First, you have to forecast where the dollar is going, which is as good as heads versus tails
  • Then, you must accurately identify what will happen to small and large caps if you get the dollar right

It's easier said than done, as I think figure 4 makes clear. Even if you accurately predict the dollar, good luck making heads or tails out of large and small because of it.

Figure 4: Predicting This Stuff Isn't Easy

I have given the "dollar vs. cap size" concept a ton of thought over the years.

The common refrain goes something like this: "If the dollar rises, that is going to be bad for big U.S. multinationals, because their costs will be in expensive dollars and their customers will pay them in weak euros and weak yen. Therefore, if I'm bullish the dollar, I should go for small caps."

If it were only that simple. Dollar up, give me small caps. Everyone knows that. But it isn't necessarily true.

How about if "dollar up" is happening because some hedge fund is blowing up or a banking institution is dying in real time? In those markets, people are doing wild things like getting diamonds into Swiss safe deposit boxes, emptying ATMs and so on. In that world, where the grab for dollars is on, you probably aren't going to be happy with small caps.

Moral of the story: like small caps because you like small caps, not because of any U.S. dollar forecast that is on your mind.

Now…that doesn't mean you can't engage in some old-fashioned Fed Kremlinology. That work could help small caps because of a narrative that figure 5 lays out. It shows a concept that is intuitive: as a collective, large caps tend to stand on sturdier balance sheet ground than small caps. Interest coverage (earnings before interest and taxes divided by interest expense) is a notably different scene as you move across the cap spectrum.

Figure 5: Interest Coverage Ratio

If the Fed starts cutting rates, that helps not only people and companies with large debt burdens, but also those who are more likely to have floating-rate obligations. Both of those descriptors apply more to small caps than large caps. It's something to consider if the Fed starts easing policy.

Figure 6 sorts our four U.S. small-cap Funds by quality (return on equity).

Figure 6: U.S. Small-Cap Fundamentals

The performance data quoted represents past performance and is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performance, click the respective ticker: DES, EES, DGRS, QSML.

Funds mentioned in figure 6:

  • DES: WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Dividend Fund
  • EES: WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Fund
  • DGRS: WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Dividend Growth Fund
  • QSML: WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Growth Fund

1 Performance figures from WisdomTree's PATH software, as of 8/27/25.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors and/or smaller companies increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Jeff Weniger, CFA, Head of Equity Strategy

Jeff Weniger, CFA, is Head of Equity Strategy at WisdomTree, where he helps shape the firm’s stock market outlook by blending macroeconomic insights with fundamental analysis. With over a decade of experience in investment strategy, Jeff previously served as Director and Senior Strategist at BMO in the office of the CIO, contributing to the Asset Allocation Committee and co-managing ETF model portfolios across the U.S. and Canada. Notably, in 2013, at age 32, Jeff became the youngest member selected for BMO’s Global Investment Forum, joining the firm’s top strategists to craft its long-term investment outlook.

Jeff holds a B.S. in Finance from the University of Florida and an MBA from Notre Dame, and has been a CFA charterholder since 2006. A recognized voice in financial media, he frequently appears on CNBC Asia, Canada’s Business News Network (BNN), and Wharton Business Radio, and contributes insights to leading outlets including Barron’s and the Wall Street Journal. Jeff also has an extensive social media following, engaging a broad audience with thoughtful insight and market perspectives.

Original Post

data.title: Oak Valley Bancorp: A Loan Book With Zero Loans Past Due
data.description:
Introduction

Oak Valley Bancorp (NASDAQ:OVLY) is the holding company for the Oak Valley Community Bank, which started operations in the early nineties. The bank currently has 18 branches, with a 19th anticipated to open in Lodi, in the San Francisco hinterland.

Seeing a bank with consistently zero loan loss provisions is pretty unique

The main reason why I’m interested in this local bank is the fact its loan loss provisions are consistently zero, which is unique in the sector. Granted, there are always banks that were able to recover previously record provisions but it's very rare to see a bank not having to record any provisions at all.

That’s the case at Oak Valley Bancorp. As you can see below on the income statement, the bank reported interest income of $21.4M, which is an increase of approximately $1.2M compared to the second quarter of 2024. And although the total interest expenses also increased, the net interest income increased by approximately 5% to $18.15M. And as the income statement below shows, the total amount of loan loss provisions was once again zero.

The bank did have to deal with higher non-interest expenses while the amount of non-interest income slightly decreased. This still resulted in a pre-tax income of $7.2M, but this is a decrease of approximately $0.25M compared to the same quarter a year before. The bottom line shows a net profit of just under $5.6M resulting in an EPS of $0.68 (and $0.67 per diluted share). This pushed the H1 2025 EPS to $1.32. The main pressure on the bank’s earnings is coming from the "other expenses" and an increase in the G&A expenses, which Oak Valley blames on the general operating costs to grow the loan book.

Oak Valley Bancorp currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share, which means its payout ratio is currently less than 50%. This allows the bank to retain the majority of its earnings on the balance sheet. This supports the (tangible) book value per share and its plans to pursue loan growth. The bank is planning to open a new branch in Lodi.

A look at the loan book: zero loans are past due

The bank currently has a total of just over $1.9B in assets on its balance sheet, with a very large portion held in liquid and very liquid assets. As you can see below, the bank holds almost $200M in cash and has invested north of $500M in securities available for sale. There are no securities held to maturity which means there's no risk of the fair value being lower than the book value.

Although in excess of 35% of the assets are held in cash and securities, the bank also has a robust loan portfolio of almost $1.1B. As you can see below, the vast majority (in excess of 80%) is classified as commercial real estate, with the majority of that subdivision classified as non-owner occupied.

While there's a general perception commercial real estate loans can be risky, it really depends on the characteristics of the loan as well. A bank can have issued office loans at an 80% LTV ratio, or it may have issued a loan for a commercial space at a 40% LTV ratio. It's thus wrong to consider all CRE loans "dangerous." Oak Valley is the perfect example of this as literally zero of the loans in its loan book are classified as past due. The bank didn’t disclose its LTV ratio in June, but its FY 2024 annual report mentions the average LTV ratio for its CRE portfolio was just 45.3% based on the most recent appraisals (which happened at the time of origination or renewal of the loans).

This explains why no additional loan loss provisions are required, as the bank’s balance sheet already contains an accumulated loan loss provision of in excess of 1% of its loan book.

Investment thesis

I like Oak Valley Bancorp for its conservative loan portfolio. It's very rare to see a bank having to record zero loan loss provisions and not having a single loan past due despite its loan portfolio being heavily concentrated on commercial real estate loans. The low LTV ratio of less than 50% is proving its value when it comes to "asset safety." I will keep an eye on the evolution of the non-interest expenses, but I expect the opening of the new branch in Lodi to be accretive to the bottom line from 2026 on.

At the end of June, the bank’s tangible book value per share was approximately $21.77, which means the stock is trading at a premium of approximately 30% to its tangible book value, but that’s a price I’m rather happy to pay for the loan book quality. Additionally, the 4.2% dividend yield provides a nice compensation while waiting for the next catalyst.

I currently have no position in Oak Valley Bancorp, but this bank is now pretty high up on my list of regional banks I’m keeping an eye on. I may initiate a long position, but this is unlikely to happen in the next 72 hours.

data.title: Roku: Amazon Partnership Vs. Looming Walmart Competition
data.description:
Roku (NASDAQ:ROKU) appears to be viewed more for its potential cyclicality rather than its exposure to the online advertising trend. That may be creating a protracted buying opportunity in the stock. The company has no shortage of potential near-term catalysts ranging between margin expansion and a potentially lucrative third-party partnership. There may be looming risks regarding a key channel, but the strong balance sheet and reasonable valuation support optimism. I reiterate my "Buy" rating for the stock.

ROKU Stock Price

I last covered ROKU in June, when I rated the stock a "Buy" because of the partnership with Amazon (AMZN). The stock has returned around 9% since then.

I see more upside ahead as the company relies on a time-proven playbook.

ROKU Stock Key Metrics

ROKU is known for its smart TV products, allowing consumers to access streaming apps on their televisions. ROKU primarily generates its business through advertisements and commission fees derived from the platform.

In the most recent quarter, ROKU delivered 15% YoY growth in net revenue to $1.111 billion, exceeding guidance of $1.070 billion. Gross profits jumped 17% YoY to $497.7 million, crushing guidance of $465 million. Adjusted EBITDA of $78.2 million also beat guidance of $70 million.

Stock-based compensation still makes up more than adjusted EBITDA, but that ratio has improved greatly. If ROKU can keep up the growth, I expect a serious push into GAAP profitability ahead.

The company ended the quarter with $2.3 billion of cash versus no debt and authorized a $400 million stock repurchase program. Looking ahead, management guided for the third quarter to see $520 million in gross profit, representing 8.3% YoY growth and $110 million in adjusted EBITDA. I suspect the market was disappointed by the growth target, but I note that the profit guide implies solid margin expansion.

On the conference call, management noted that their deal with AMZN was “not exclusive” while also once again downplaying the potential threat from Walmart (WMT) given their acquisition of Vizio. I suspect that investors are also unconvinced regarding management’s lack of concern, especially given that WMT might account for as much as a third of ROKU’s device sales, according to one analyst in 2024. Management did note that they expect the impact from their partnership with AMZN to hit in the fourth quarter at the earliest. Put another way, investors have something to look forward to in terms of a growth catalyst heading into the next year.

Is ROKU Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

As of recent prices, ROKU traded hands at just around 7x gross profits. Consensus estimates call for low-double-digit to high-single-digit top-line growth over the coming years.

The bullish thesis is a bit messy, as benefits from the AMZN partnership might be offset by rising risk from the WMT channel. Based on 30% long-term net margins, the stock trades at roughly 23x long-term earnings. I view this valuation as being distinctly not “dirt cheap” but looking more than reasonable given the net cash balance sheet, positive cash flow generation, as well as the exposure to the secular growth story of online advertising. I suspect that once the company breaks into GAAP profitability, which I estimate to happen within the next four quarters, then the stock will earn serious valuation support and enable it to deliver annual returns in line or in excess of its top-line growth rate.

ROKU Stock Risks

It is possible that ROKU is being disrupted, first by Vizio, then by others. As a consumer, all these streaming apps and platforms feel very interchangeable. It is possible that ROKU over-invests in attempts to overcome that interchangeability. ROKU’s business is sensitive to market health and may face volatility in the event of a market downturn. The stock is not “dirt cheap” and may also face volatility in a selloff. Tariffs may increase the production costs of the product and may hurt cash flow even if investors tend to focus more on the platform's gross profits.

ROKU Stock Conclusion

ROKU still looks like a reasonable bet as a key player in the shift to online advertising. Investors need to look out for potential weakness due to WMT, but I expect the AMZN partnership to deliver a surprise boost come 2026. The company continues to expand profit margins and maintains a net cash balance sheet. ROKU shares many of the traits that I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Metaplanet's M/NAV Hangover And Why The Stack Keeps Climbing
data.description:
Back in early July, we walked through Metaplanet’s (MTPLF) monumental shift from quirky hotels to Asia’s flagship Bitcoin Treasury Company. We talked about the “moving-strike” warrants strategy, zero-coupon bonds, the “555 Million Plan,” and how raising equity at a premium to net asset value [NAV] could keep BTC per share compounding. That was the very flywheel. The somewhat similar flywheel previously embraced by Strategy (MSTR) that Metaplanet now "localised" to Japan. Since then, however, the story has added some grit, as even though the company’s bitcoin pile is meaningfully larger today, the stock is down 45% since my article went out. A pretty disheartening result for a two-month period.

What actually drove the share price slide?

The clearest explanation is persistent m/NAV compression, or a compression in the market multiple paid over Metaplanet’s per-share BTC NAV, which has shrunk significantly.

As you can see from the company's official analytics in the chart, in June and early July, when I published my initial piece, investors were paying several times the NAV to front-run future BTC/share accretion. Through late summer, the premium cooled even as the firm continued to buy bitcoin, hence the multiple compression BTC is trading at the same levels as two months ago.

Meanwhile, the BTC stack didn’t stop

But did Metaplanet really achieve anything meaningful during this period? Yes, because they didn't stop accumulating. Even if a softer multiple made it increasingly less attractive to issue equity to buy BTC, it still made sense to do so, as it is accretive on a BTC/share basis.

Since my July article, when the balance stood at 13,350 BTC, Metaplanet’s buys have been relentless, with today's (Sep.1) disclosure pushing its total holdings to 20,000 BTC.

But more importantly, these BTC buys were quite accretive on a per-share basis, which is the whole foundation of Metaplanet's investment case against, say, buying a BTC ETF. As you can see, Metaplanet now owns ₿0.02111503 per 1,000 shares, up from ₿0.01595568 on July 1st. That's a 32.3% increase in BTC/share in just two months, which also goes to show why it makes sense that the stock trades at a premium, and is likely to continue to do so.

Q2 was also the first quarter where the “Bitcoin Income Generation” line really showed up in the P&L, as management reported ¥1,239 million ($8.4 million) in revenue and ¥817 million ($5.56 million) in operating profit, with ¥1,131 million ($7.7 million) of that revenue coming from cash-secured BTC puts.

The strategy didn’t change; its funding mix did

Now, even though Metaplanet did achieve excellent per-share accretion on its BTC holdings, you can clearly see in the chart that this pace is still nowhere near the pace between the start of the year and June. Of course, the softer multiple explains that. It is still accretive to BTC/share to raise capital close to 2x NAV to buy BTC, but not as much as it is at 6x NAV.

In response to this "problem," just a few days ago, on August 27, Metaplanet’s board approved an international share offering targeting ¥130.3 billion (~$880 million). The company’s notice hints, as we would expect, that most proceeds are earmarked for buying more bitcoin. In the same batch of disclosures, the company temporarily suspended exercises of several moving-strike warrant series, which again is a nod to today’s tighter premium and them seeking more accretive BTC stacking options.

The overseas offering is a big deal because it will broaden the investor base beyond the EVO-driven warrant ecosystem that worked brilliantly when local demand (and m/NAV) was roaring. A marketed international deal should let Metaplanet tap deeper pools of long-only and crossover capital, set a clearing price through bookbuilding, and reduce reliance on the day-to-day warrant exercises to fund BTC purchases, which could re-accelerate treasury growth.

The company also pushed forward a two-year shelf to issue up to ¥555 billion of perpetual preferred shares. In its Q2 presentation, management positions the prefs as BTC-backed credit designed for Japan’s enormous (and, as I explained in my initial article, underutilized) fixed-income market. If approved and launched, that should be a structural way to scale holdings even when the common stock premium wobbles.

Strategy has adopted the same philosophy by building a yield curve across STRF (STRF), STRD (STRD), STRK (STRK), and STRC (STRC), so this could be a similar issuance journey for Metaplanet. In fact, given that Japan is starved of fixed income and income-generating preferred shares, Metaplanet's instruments could prove wildly successful among regional investors.

A quiet but material tailwind: Index inclusion

One more piece that didn’t grab as many headlines is that, last week, FTSE Russell upgraded Metaplanet to mid-cap and added it to the FTSE Japan Index in the September review. This will pull in passive flows that otherwise probably wouldn't have touched a niche Bitcoin treasury name, especially relevant in a market where local, plain-vanilla spot BTC vehicles are limited. This factor, too, should broaden the shareholder base and, potentially, support a higher NAV premium as passive inflows add to the stock's buying volumes.

So… was the drawdown a thesis break or a reset?

If your thesis was “Metaplanet can keep raising at a 6-10x premium forever,” then sure, the past couple of months have been tough. But, if your thesis was “Metaplanet will keep growing its BTC stack and build multiple ways to fund that stack,” the facts line up. The company continues to aggressively stack BTC, increasing the per-share BTC value, even in a challenging market for its equity.

Now it's true that none of this removes the risks. A stubbornly light premium could make the flywheel less accretive for a while, bitcoin’s volatility can swamp any quarter, and the preferred program could go south, for whatever reason, eroding hopes for faster BTC/share accumulation.

But if you buy the core idea that Metaplanet is the primary listed proxy for bitcoin exposure in Tokyo and is deliberately plugging into Japan’s enormous savings and fixed-income base, I feel that the m/NAV compression reads like an opportunity. Lower entry multiples today, more diversified funding tomorrow, and a treasury that (so far) keeps getting bigger support the bullish story.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Seagate Technology: Ahead In Technology, Behind In Growth
data.description:
Seagate Technology (NASDAQ:STX) has soared nearly 3x since the April lows, with an increase in demand for high-capacity storage for AI workloads as well as heat-assisted magnetic recording (HAMR) storage, which is advancing fast. With cyclicality favouring Seagate, it is running off huge momentum, but the trend needs to show that it is able to break past its all-time high revenues back in 2022, as the storage market seems to be rebounding since 2024. Margins are expanding compared to years prior, but the high debt, relative valuation and high relative strength index suggest that the potential upside and growth trajectory for future years is unclear with high cyclicality. Hence, I'm rating Seagate at a 'Hold'.

Thesis

The AI boom isn't slowing down yet, as recent earnings waves solidified the AI boom as firms like Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) increased CAPEX by $10B despite times of uncertainty, when many firms are setting aside funds due to macroeconomic headwinds. McKinsey also estimated that global investment in AI infrastructure will hit $7 trillion by 2030, as the AI boom doesn't slow down.

Seagate provide hard driver disks (HDDs), with hyperscalers relying on HDDs with over 90% of exabytes of cloud data stored on HDDs according to Seagate (1 exabyte = 1 Billion GB). this shows that HDDs are significant in AI storage and required in this AI boom where more and more data centers are being built worldwide, with top hyperscalers like Google, Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT) spending hundreds of billions of capex. Seagate benefits with more and more data centers being built with the rise of cloud, where revenues for these segments are growing and growing fast (recent quarter Amazon 17% YoY, Google 32% YoY and Microsoft 39% YoY). Further to that, Seagate's next-generation HAMR-based HDDs further improve energy efficiency and are essential for AI workloads especially with the current increased scale, as Seagate is an important part of this AI boom.

However, compared to its closest peer, Western Digital (WDC), it is slightly overvalued and overbought on the relative strength index on a 5-year scale. Despite margin expansions, it is not hitting all-time high revenues, as we are in the era of the AI boom and a rebound all the way to all-time highs is needed to show that the peak of the cyclicality is higher than before. If the cyclicality does start again with falling revenues, Seagate could be in trouble with its $5.37B in debt which is not a problem now, but a downturn mixed with high debt is a huge red flag. Hence, I'm rating Seagate at a 'Hold'.

Seagate's Business

Seagate's main revenue driver seen above is HDD, with 93% of revenue coming from HDDs. Seagate also provides solid state drives (SSDs) but the segment is significantly smaller than HDD. Legacy refers to older and declining products that are becoming obsolete because of new inventions.

HDDs are mechanical storage devices that use disks to store data. SSDs a solid-state storage that uses integrated circuits to store data. It is way faster to access data with SSD than with HDD as there are no moving parts (disks would spin constantly), but HDD is way cheaper compared to SSD on a price per terabyte basis, estimated at over 6x cheaper. With hyperscalers wanting to cut operational costs (Variable costs) as much as possible, using HDDs reduces costs for large-scale AI and cloud workloads. AI processing output expected sizes are hundreds of terabytes (1000GB = 1TB) or even petabytes (1,048,576GB = 1PB). With investors putting huge premiums on hyperscalers and AI giants, alongside huge revenue growths, their margins also have to be bottom-line expanding, so HDDs are the preferred method with lower costs and over 90% of cloud data is on HDD as mentioned in the thesis.

Seagate often has the best hard drives, with a source stating they are the best HDD for most use cases. Seagate is still leading in HDD, with its 36TB announced back in January 2025. They are the first 36TB HAMR HDDs that are brought to the market, with refurbished HDDs selling near their brand new price which shows Seagate's leadership in HDDs. Western Digital plans for 36TB HAMR HDDs would only start shipping in 2027, which shows that Seagate is ahead by 2 whole years.

Shingled magnetic recording (SMR) is a magnetic storage data used in HDDs to increase storage density and overall per-drive storage capacity. SMR tends to be preferred by SMR as it has a higher density and lower cost per TB.

Conventional Magnetic Recording (CMR) is a technology for data recording on magnetic media. This is prefered for mixed and random workloads due to better performance but has a lower density and higher cost per TB.

The idea from the graph is rather straightforward: Seagate has leadership in HDDs, as Western Digital trails behind. Assuming Western Digital's target of 36TB shipping in 2027, this suggests that Seagate is leading by 2 years with Seagate's launch back in January 2025. For the most advanced HDDs, Seagate would be the favourite especially for the most advanced AI workloads. However, there will be competition in lower models with high price competition as older products would have little differentiation amongst all the firms.

HDD and HAMR market growth isn't slowing down either. It is projected to grow with a CAGR of 5.3% and 16.5% respectively until 2033 and 2035. If the HDD market continues to grow as projected and break out to all time high revenues, Seagate does look attractive with the constant increase in capex by hyperscalers (caught in prisoner dilemma, if 1 increases the other has to as well so they don't fall behind and have slower growth), as the demand for storage continues to show strength in 2025 with the recent quarter having 32% YoY growth for HDDs and 40% YoY for mass capacity HDDs (most up to date HDDs).

Cyclicality

Seagate is affected by cyclicality as we are in the AI boom, which led to a run-up in stock price as HDD demand is expected to go up. Despite the AI boom, Seagate also has yet to break to all-time high revenues which most AI giants are posting quarterly, which suggests that if the cyclicality doesn't break out to all-time high revenues, each height of the cyclicality is falling as HDDs are no longer needed with the market being saturated with it. There will also be competition in lower models with high price competition as older products would have little differentiation amongst all the firms.

In 2022, they posted revenue of $11.66B, with $7.38B in 2023 and negative net margins. It was noted mid 2023 that there is a 'profound downturn in demand' by the CEO with a fall in hyperscalers buying fewer nearline drivers. This exactly shows how volatile the AI scene is, as with the change of ideas of hyperscalers in just a few months sent revenue fell by ~36.7% in 1 year, alongside negative net income for the year. Seagate is way more prepared now shown with higher net margins, but caution will always remain as the volatility for Seagate is high as soon as hyperscalers change the amount of capex they are willing to spend.

This is also seen with other companies that are reliant on the AI boom, using Dell for servers in this scenario. In Q1 FY26, Dell's orders popped up higher than all of FY25 combined, which I believe indicates that companies are pulling forward their orders to avoid as much tariff costs as possible. Orders continue to remain strong in Q2 FY26 with solid growth YoY of 75%, but it is quite a significant drop from Q1 to Q2.

Though in their earnings call they noted:

Based on our current outlook, we expect minimal tariff-related impacts to the business.

This suggests that additional tariff costs may weigh less on Seagate than other companies which are setting aside funds, but because of the potential additional costs, firms may be pulling orders forward for storage to hold on to avoid these costs. These suggest that the recent revenue growth of 30% YoY may not be sustainable, as hyperscalers are just ordering earlier to avoid tariff costs. The idea is that the cost in their balance sheet is not increasing because of tariffs, but the revenue number may be distorted and higher because hyperscalers are pulling orders forward.

With a market cap of $35.6B at the time of writing and $5.37B debt, this could be an issue if revenue shows any sign of weakness with the market being cyclical again. If revenues fall to 2024 levels of $6.5B with net income of only 5% for 2024, this could be a huge issue for Seagate in the long term. Revenue seems to be doing fine now, but such high debt alongside falling revenues is not a good sign.

Operating margins are improving at 17.3% in Q4 FY24 and now at 26.2%, which is over 50% expansion. They will be much more improved with the debt as of now with better margins, but remain cautious if margins contract and revenues start to fall.

Competiton

Despite Seagate having leadership in top HDDs, it still closely competes with Western Digital. In Q1 from Forbes,

Seagate said their HDD revenue was $2,003B in the quarter with Mass Capacity HDDs accounting for 87% of this revenue (as was the case in the prior two quarters). Total storage capacity shipped was 143.6EB with 132.9TB for all Mass Capacity and 119.7TB for Nearline HDDs, the balance of 10.7TB was shipped for legacy applications. The average HDD capacity was 12.6TB with Mass Capacity average capacity at 16.2TB and legacy average capacity at 3.3TB.

Western Digital also said that they shipped 1.9M client compute units, consumer shipped units were 1.9M and data center (cloud HDD unit shipments) were 8.3M. That means the average capacity of their nearline HDDs was about 17.5TB.

Note: Seagate calls it high-capacity drives Mass Capacity HDDs, whilst Western Digital calls it Nearline HDDs

This showed that despite leadership in top models, Seagate falls behind when it comes to the worst models of advanced HDDs. The average mass capacity HDDs shipped were 16.2TB for Seagate, whilst Western Digital's nearline HDDs were 17.5TB. The average suggests that Western Digital isn't that far behind and it is likely that Seagate sells its worst models better than its better models, whilst Western Digital is the opposite with a higher average.

This isn't because Seagate sells at scale either, as Western Digital in Q1 actually had a higher market share than Seagate. We are now in Q3 analysing Q2 results, with Seagate Mass Capacity HDDs reporting $2B in revenue, whilst Western Digital reported $2.3B in revenue, showing that Western Digital is favoured among enterprises for models worst than the 36TB that Seagate has and Western Digital is planning to ship by 2027. The script could flip for Seagate with its most advanced model, but with its outlook for Q3:

Accounting for these factors and for the 14-week period, we expect September quarter revenue to be in the range of $2.5 billion, plus or minus $150 million. At the midpoint, this reflects a 15% improvement year-over-year.

A 15% YoY revenue isn't bad, but disappointing compared to the last 4 quarters of 50% 50% 30% 30% revenue growth. Western Digital also had its outlook for a revenue growth of 22% YoY for a revenue of $2.7Bm which is more impressive than Seagate. Seagate has competition, and they aren't falling behind whilst growing faster in Q3, which I argue is a concern for Seagate investors since Seagate trades at a higher multiple than Western Digital. Western Digital's debt problem is also a lot better than Seagate with more cash, as they have $2.5B in cash and $4.85B in debt, way better managing than Seagate with higher revenues. Seagate's recent operating margin posted to be 26.2%, whilst Western Digital was at 28.1%, so despite expanding margins, it still has a worse margin than its closest peer.

Solid Dividend Yield

Seagate has an attractive dividend of 1.7%, as not many AI/technology firms offer a dividend higher than 1% as investors expect dividends to be reinvested into the business. The Mag7, Broadcom and AMD just to name a have a dividend yield of lower than 1% or simply don't offer one at all, as people buy into AI stocks for the growth. With a blend of growth and dividend income, this could be attractive for investors who want a small income quarterly. Seagate also offers a higher dividend yield than Western Digital, whose forward yield is sitting around 0.5% right now.

Valuation

This is Seagate's Valuation Metrics

On a P/E non-GAAP and GAAP basis to its relative sector, it trades lower than the sector median, which could suggest undervaluation. It trades over 15% P/S to the sector median, which could suggest overvaluation relative to the sector. Except for forward P/E non-GAAP, P/S and P/E TTM are also trading higher than historical averages which could signal overvaluation.

Comparing P/E and P/S with Western Digital:

On both the P/E and P/S basis compared to Western Digital, it seems that it is overvalued. On a P/E basis, to even get close to Western Digital's level, it would have to correct by over 25%. On a P/S basis, it would have to correct over 25% as well to get to Western's Digital P/S.

Western Digital currently has higher revenue, better operating margins. Higher revenue growth rates whilst Seagate has the highest TB HHDs in the market. I argue that the best product on the market does justify a premium, but it doesn't justify a premium of over 25% if Western Digital has better metrics across the board. Western Digital's debt situation is also handled better than Seagate at this moment with Seagate having $900M in cash and $5.37B debt whilst Western Digital has $2.47B cash and $4.85B debt.

This could be seen as Western Capital being undervalued rather than Seagate being overvalued considering the fact that they are already below sector median, but I would rather be cautious and believe it is overvalued because of its cyclicality.

Conclusion

Seagate needs to show that its cyclicality is able to hit new highs especially breaking out to all-time highs, as doubts will be in investors' minds with the huge falloff of revenues in 2023 and 2024, showing instability for the HDD provider stocks. Its premium is also higher than Western Digital's, which is why for now I'm staying on the sidelines for Seagate and remaining cautious especially the near 3x return since its April bottom.

data.title: Hamilton Insurance Group: Pricing In Strong Fundamentals (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of the Hamilton Insurance Group (HG) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 23%. While much of the insurance sector has faced pressure over the last quarter or so amidst fear of slowing premium growth, HG has been able to buck the trend, trading near a 52-week high. Strong results and a favorable hurricane season have helped to buoy the stock. Indeed, since I last covered Hamilton in June, rating shares a buy, the stock has gained 14%, outpacing the market’s 8% rally. Shares are now at my $23.50-$24 interim price target, making it a good time to revisit HG.

There has been increasing concern in the industry about weakening pricing trends. After years of strong premium inflation enabled insurers to recapture margin, we have seen premium inflation steadily slow, which likely means the insurance industry’s underwriting margin has hit a peak. That said, premium inflation, in aggregate, is still keeping pace with inflation. As such, I am not yet concerned about a material compression in insurance margins or earnings power.

Indeed, results continue to be healthy. In the company’s second quarter, reported on August 6th, Hamilton earned $1.55, blowing away estimates by $0.48. Its combined ratio was an excellent 86.8%, leading to $67 million of underwriting income. This was up from 84.4% last year, primarily due to slightly higher catastrophe losses. Still, the level of catastrophes remains fairly low. As a reminder, Hamilton is a diversified niche insurer that operates out of Bermuda, primarily focused on casualty and specialty lines, though property is also 25% of its business. It is fairly balanced between insurance and reinsurance activity.

That said, we are seeing some pockets of weakness. It is pulling back from cyber where pricing is less attractive. Excess & Surplus (E&S) also faces pressure whereas it sees attractive terms in casualty, which is rhetoric very different from peers like Everest Group (EG), though some of its challenges have been idiosyncratic. Its reinsurance renewal saw “some” rate pressure, which is similar to what other players have been saying, though the absolute level of pricing remains attractive. As such, the company used the midyear renewal to increase its property catastrophe exposure. So far, the US hurricane season has been exceptionally quiet. This should be very supportive of Q3 results, though there can be damaging storms in late September or October, which would impact Q4. Still, it appears the US hurricane season will be better than average.

Hamilton is also enjoying idiosyncratic factors that may be supporting pricing and growth. In Q2, gross premiums were up by 18% to $712 million. Being upgraded by AM Best supported $50 million of premium growth. With improved credit ratings, Hamilton is a more attractive reinsurance partner and eligible for more platforms, supporting incremental growth opportunities and potential pricing power. The company is well on track to reach its $80 million premium growth goal off this upgrade, as outlined on the earnings call.

Looking at segment results, Hamilton’s International unit saw its combined ratio improve by 1.7% to 89.3%, thanks to prior year favorable developments. Net premiums were up 17% to $253 million. Conversely, its Bermuda unit saw the combined ratio increase by 6.9% to 84.3%, given a mix shift towards casualty and one large reinsurance loss related to the Air India crash (a genuine one-off in my view). I still view a sub-85% combined ratio as excellent. Premiums were up over 25% to $258 million, aided by the AM Best upgrade.

Alongside a solid underwriting quarter, Hamilton reported excellent investment results with $149 million of net investment income, on its $5.3 billion investment portfolio. Hamilton has a unique investment portfolio. About 61% is invested in traditional fixed income and cash. This portfolio is highly liquid and very high quality, with an Aa3 average rating and 3.4-year duration. The portfolio has a 4.3% book yield. Given Fed rate cuts but positive fixed reinvestment, I see the portfolio yield being fairly similar to current levels over the next ~12 months.

Where Hamilton stands out is its 39% allocation to seven different investment vehicles managed by Two Sigma. It will always hold the lesser of $1.8 billion or 60% of its net tangible assets with the firm. Right now, it has $2.1 billion, so it can withdraw $300 million at any time, and it can redeem with just five days notice. About 70% of its funds are in equities, and over the long term performance has been strong at 13%. This hedge fund allocation is outsized relative to peers, but it has been a meaningful driver of value. Importantly, while this portion of the portfolio is relatively risky, its fixed income portfolio is very conservative, so HG is unlikely to ever be a forced redeemer during a downturn to pay claims.

This part of Hamilton’s portfolio had an annualized return in Q2 of about 17%, generating $87 million of income. Q2 was extremely volatile, offering outsized return opportunities, and this is well in excess of long-term averages. These results can vary quarter by quarter, and thanks to the outsized gain, HG reported a 30% annualized return on equity. Assuming a ~10% long-term return in Two Sigma, investment income would have been about $30 million lower, or about ~$0.25 towards EPS.

When valuing HG, it is important to use a run-rate investment income view, rather than be swung around by each quarter’s return, in my view. Still, thanks to this strong performance, book value is now $25.55. This enabled $35 million of buybacks in the quarter, followed by $15 million in July. $62 million remains on its authorization. Management continues to expect double-digit premium growth, but at a slowing pace. This should enable ongoing buybacks.

Now, in the long term, I still expect HG’s combined ratio to run in the low-to-mid 90% area, assuming a normal catastrophe load, though I expect Hamilton to outperform that in Q3. As such, I believe shares should trade at least 1x book value to 1.1x book value, though the opacity around its investment portfolio likely limits upside beyond that. At a ~$26.25 fair value, shares have about 9% upside.

Accordingly, I am moving shares to a “hold.” I see sufficient upside to continue performing in line with the market. However, given their rally and outperformance vs. peers, I do not see enough upside to maintain a “buy” rating, as future returns are more likely to be market-like. On a pullback towards $23, I would be a buyer, but for now, there is no rush to add HG.

data.title: Anheuser-Busch InBev's Selloff Overly Done, Albeit Expected - Secular Transition At Play
data.description:
BUD Remains A Compelling Beverage Play, Thanks To Its Robust Profitability & Discounted Valuations

We previously covered Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD) (OTCPK:BUDFF) in June 2025, discussing how the worst of its prior boycott might already be behind us, with FQ1'25 results merely attributed to seasonal factors / industry-wide headwinds and similar YoY declines observed in its peers.

Combined with the bottoming owned beer volumes sold, the robust Beyond Beer/ no-alcohol beer performance, and the renewed growth prospects from its diversified portfolios, we had reiterated our Buy rating upon a moderate correction nearer to its next support levels of between $58s and $66s ranges, attributed to the overbought technical indicators then.

BUD 1Y Stock Price

Since then, BUD has indeed pulled back as discussed in our last article, with the bulls defending the $57s floor by early August 2025 and the stock trading sideways at the $60s ranges since then.

Much of the headwinds are attributed to the market's unmet expectations against the underwhelming FQ2'25 performance, as the brewer misses the top-line estimates and just barely meets the bottom-line estimates, worsened by the notable YTD overall volume decline by -2% YoY, the beer volume decline by -2.3% YoY, and the stagnant non-beer volume performance at 0% YoY.

Even so, we believe that the stock sell-off by -14.1% at its worst has been overly done indeed, since BUD's headwinds are merely attributed to the "soft industries and performance in China and Brazil."

BUD's Volume Growth Profile On A QoQ/ YoY Basis

For example, FQ2'25 has brought forth robust volume growth in the North American region by +12.7% QoQ by absolute numbers/ +0.3% YoY organically, the Middle Americas by +10.6% QoQ/ +1.1% YoY, and the EMEA by +16.4% QoQ/ +0.9% YoY, respectively.

These numbers otherwise imply an overall volume growth by absolute numbers by +12.8% QoQ/ +1.7% YoY (after discounting the South American/ APAC regions), with it underscoring the brewer's relatively healthy performance thus far.

This is on top of the fact that BUD's revenue headwinds in Brazil by -1.9% YoY is partly attributed to "adverse weather conditions," the "weakening of mass-market beer portfolios in Brazil, a trend affecting the entire industry," and the "complex economic environment and brewers shifting more focus to premium brands," as similarly reported by Heineken N.V. (OTCQX:HEINY), with it underscoring an industry wide headwind.

A similarly pessimistic trend has been observed in China as well, "as consumers reprioritise value, casual occasions and relevance over prestige" during a sluggish economic market, with International Wine and Spirits Record [IWSR] already expecting:

While beer volumes between 2024 and 2034 are predicted to be flat, RTDs are expected to grow at a CAGR of +2%, sparkling wine at a CAGR of +5% and still wine flat. Spirits are expected to decline at a CAGR of -1%, but excluding baijiu the 10-year volume CAGR is +2%. (IWSR)

Combined with BUD already reporting a declining revenue in China by -6.2% YoY, we can understand why the market has opted to sell first and ask later, attributed to potential growth headwinds arising from two key countries.

On the other hand, it is apparent that the management's ongoing Beyond Beer/ no-alcohol and premiumization strategy have paid off handsomely, as observed in the robust overall no-alcohol beer revenue growth by +33% YoY in FQ2'25, the richer overall adj EBITDA margins of 35.5% (+1.1 points YoY), and the excellent adj EPS performance at $0.98 (+17.4% YoY on a constant currency basis).

These developments underscore that while BUD may continue to face headwinds in select countries, its global megabrand portfolios are likely to remain highly profitable moving forward.

This is especially since the management has already highlighted numerous initiatives to drive renewed growth in Brazil through premiumization and in China through the intensified penetration in the in-home channel while leveraging on its BEES digital sales platform (where over 70% of its domestic revenues are based on).

These strategies may allow the brewer to increasingly ramp up its sales growth as they also scale up the "key innovations such as Harbin Zero Sugar," allowing them to tap into China's booming non-alcoholic market size growth from $169B in 2024 to $291B in 2033, expanding at a CAGR of +6.2%.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Otherwise, pending growth materialization, we can understand why the consensus forward estimates appear to be rather mixed, with BUD expected to report a decelerating top/ bottom-line growth profile at a CAGR of +3%/ +12.3% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +5.2%/ +14.6% a year ago, albeit still much improved compared to the 5Y historical growth profile at +2.7%/ -2.9% and the 10Y growth at +2.4%/ -4.2%, respectively.

BUD Valuations

The expanded top/ bottom-line growth profile is also why we believe that BUD appears to be cheaply valued at FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 9.42x and the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 16.55x, compared to the 5Y mean of 10.33x/ 20.15x and the 10Y mean of 11.61x/ 20.55x, respectively.

This is especially since BUD's FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.76x is inherently discounted compared to the 5Y mean of 2.78x, the 10Y mean of 4.89x, and some of its brewery/ beverage/ energy drinks peers, including:

  • Constellation Brands (STZ) at 3.36x,
  • The Boston Beer Company (SAM) at 1.95x,
  • Molson Coors Beverage (TAP) at 9.29,
  • PepsiCo (PEP) at 5.04x,
  • Coca-Cola Company (KO) at 4.11x,
  • Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) at 1.82x,
  • Monster Beverage (MNST) at 2.18x, and
  • Celsius Holding (CELH) at 1.25x.

This development exemplifies why BUD continues to offer a compelling growth investment thesis for those looking for resilient, consumer-related stocks, especially given that its ability to increasingly lean into the non-beer market to drive renewed growth as consumer tastes change.

While anecdotal, we have turned to zero alcohol/ zero carb beers for great taste without the hangover/ calorie intake issues, with it building upon our increased awareness of alcohol health impacts as well.

Given the "changing attitudes about alcohol" and the moderating "number of (alcoholic) drinks consumed over the past seven days is 2.8, the lowest figure Gallup has recorded since 1996" in the US, it is unsurprising that BUD has been able to report "high twenties" volume growth in the no-alcohol beer segment in the US.

This is attributed to the popularity of Michelob Ultra Zero and Busch Light Apple, along with "low-twenties volume growth" in the Beyond Beer segment, attributed to the robust demand for spirits-based Ready To Drink Cutwater and Nütrl.

With the International Wine and Spirits Record [IWSR] expecting the "US nonalcoholic volume to gain 16% a year over the next decade," we believe that BUD appears to be well positioned to weather the industry's secular transition over the next few challenging years.

So, Is BUD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

BUD 5Y Stock Price

For now, BUD has continued to trade sideways between the established resistance levels of $73s and the support levels of $44s ranges since the worst of the COVID-19 pandemic in early 2020s.

This cadence is naturally disheartening, with the stock trading nearer to our base-case fair-value estimates of $59.20, based on the annualized H1'25 adj EPS of $3.58 and the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 16.55x.

Even so, given the accelerated adj EBITDA growth by +7.2% in H1'25, adj EPS growth by +18.7% YoY in H1'25 on a constant currency basis, and the management's reiterated FY2025 adj "EBITDA to grow in line with our medium-term outlook of between 4-8%," we believe that the BUD stock deserves an upward re-rating nearer to the peer group P/E mean of ~22x (not too far from the 10Y P/E mean of 20.55x), with it implying its inherent undervaluation against our bull-case fair value estimates of $78.70.

A similar conclusion may also be derived against our bull-case long-term price target of $109.70, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $4.99 and the peer group P/E mean of ~22x, with it suggesting an excellent near doubling upside potential of +75% ahead.

While minimal, BUD continues to offer a decent forward dividend yield of 1.80% as well, with the sideways trading cadence already triggering an improved return profile against the 4Y mean of 1.27%, thanks to the 3Y dividend per share growth rate at +28.8% albeit yet to match the pre-pandemic payout levels.

Even so, we believe that further dividend hikes are likely, since the brewer is increasingly nearer to its net debt to adj EBITDA target ratio of 3x, based on the 3.27x observed in FQ2'25, down from 3.42x in FQ2'24 and 4x in FY2019, with the management likely to engage in further shareholder returns as observed in the -0.7% shares retired over the LTM.

At the same time, readers must note that the healthier balance sheet has already triggered the moderating annualized FQ2'25 interest expenses of $2.65B (-8% YoY/ -28.9% from FY2019 levels of $3.73B) and subsequently, been adj EPS performance accretive (aided by the moderating share count).

As a result of the well balanced investment thesis across capital appreciation and shareholder returns (across dividend incomes and share buybacks), we are reiterating our Buy rating for the BUD stock here.

data.title: Lear: Trading For A Discount, But Too Many Unknowns
data.description:
Company Description

Lear Corporation (LEA) designs and manufactures automotive seats, electrical distribution systems, and electronic modules for car manufacturers. The company has operations in nearly 40 countries and has two reportable segments: Seating and E-Systems. Lear has a current market cap just shy of $6B and employs close to 175,000 people worldwide. The stock is very cyclical, as you can see from the chart below, where multiple times the stock would've returned more than 100% over the past decade (or less). However, the stock has struggled over the last few years, driving down its total return to 25.6% in the last ten years, and a resulting CAGR of just 2.3%.

Quality Financial Metrics

Let's take a look at some important financial metrics and assess Lear's performance in recent years. As usual, I'll be looking for a growing revenue stream, a stable gross profit margin, and a healthy return on invested capital.

The revenue per share for Lear has grown at a solid 6.6% since 2015, but that growth hasn't been linear. The company saw high-single-digit growth during the first couple years of the chart, before a more than 16% increase in FY17. The RPS was flat in the following two years, before the pandemic struck and sank the RPS by nearly 12%. Following that decline, Lear saw revenue growth of close to or above 10%, until 2024, when it rose just 4%. It's worth noting Lear has a share repurchase program, and on multiple occasions during the last ten years, it has reduced its share count by around 5% in a single year.

The gross profit margin was around 10 or 11 percent during the first half of the chart. In 2019, though, it shrank to just 8.8%, and fell further during the height of the pandemic. This metric hasn't recovered to its pre-pandemic levels, and from 2021 through 2024, it has averaged about 7%.

The return on invested capital metric followed a similar path to the GPM, where, prior to COVID, this metric was strong, and routinely close to or above 20%. Again, when the pandemic struck, the ROIC tanked and came in at just 4.3%, and it hasn't rebounded since that decline. I usually look for companies with an ROIC near 20%, so although Lear is capable of eclipsing that mark, it hasn't done so for about half a decade.

Dividend Data

Lear Corporation currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.77 per share, or $3.08 annually, giving it a forward dividend yield of 2.8%. Unfortunately, the pandemic threw a wrench in LEA's operations, and the dividend suffered. The company has not raised its dividend since 2021...and actually that year it hiked it from $0.25 to $0.50 in September, and then again to $0.77 in December, where it is now. Management has stated they are committed to returning cash to shareholders, but also want to remain focused on investing in the business to sustain more profitable growth. For the time being, it doesn't sound like investors will be rewarded with a dividend increase, so it's likely any excess cash will be spent on share purchases.

Earnings Update

In late July, the company announced their Q2 earnings with a double-beat, Non-GAAP earnings per share came in $0.17 above estimates at $3.47, on revenue of $6.03B, which also exceeded the consensus by more than $100M. Additionally, GAAP earnings per share was $3.06, surpassing analyst expectations by $0.23.

The Seating segment saw a small year-over-year increase in revenue, from $4.45B to $4.47B. This segment benefited from foreign exchange rates, which positively impacted sales by $53M, along with new customers, which contributed approximately $80M. Partially offsetting those numbers was a reduction in the volume of Lear platforms.

The E-Systems segment incurred a non-material decline in sales of less than $10M. An overall decline in production volume impacted these sales the most, but was partially offset by new customers and favorable foreign exchange rates.

The market had a very negative reaction to the earnings call, with shares tanking more than 8% following the release. However, since the call, shares of Lear have recovered, and are up about 1.5%. The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

The Q3 earnings for Lear are expected to be released on Friday, October 24th, and analysts are expecting non-GAAP EPS of $2.70 per share, with GAAP EPS of $2.06, on revenue of $5.55B. Interestingly, there are 11 analysts that cover Lear, and over the past 90 days, six analysts have revised their EPS estimates higher, while the remaining five have lowered their estimates.

Valuation

The last consideration is whether or not Lear is trading for an attractive valuation, and to determine this, we'll examine it from a free cash flow perspective. Additionally, to give a little more context on how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV.

The custom valuation tool suggests LEA is trading for a decent-sized discount to its fair value, with a current price of about $110, and a fair price estimated at $152 per share. The free cash flow has fluctuated pretty significantly from $1.41 per share in 2021, to almost $6.50 in 2022, and rising even more in 2023 to more than $10.50 per share. The FCF was essentially flat last fiscal year as it came in at $10.48. As you can see by the black line, the share price is well-below the valuation ranges, indicating the aforementioned discount to fair value.

The long-term expected rate of return of Lear Corporation is a robust 17.43%, with the components of that estimate being a forward dividend yield of 2.80%, a return to fair value factor of 5.06%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 9.57%.

Final Thoughts

Over the long term, this stock has struggled quite a bit, but if an investor correctly timed the market, they could've done well with it, although that's not a method of investing I recommend. The financials for Lear were much stronger prior to the pandemic, and haven't quite rebounded since that economic downturn. Moreover, the dividend has been frozen for a few years now, and although the company has a low enough payout ratio to increase their dividend, that seems unlikely for the foreseeable future. The most recent earnings call was rather mute, as neither segment saw any material changes in their numbers. The best part about this company is the valuation, as it appears to be trading at a significant discount; however, the weak earnings report, coupled with a lack of dividend growth and somewhat declining financials, makes my thoughts mixed. I think a HOLD rating is appropriate, as I'd like to see how the Q3 results come in before potentially giving this stock a rating upgrade (or downgrade).
